summary: Today's sweep leans toward Democracy / press freedom, Security / computing, Games / criticism. The strongest items in this run are Anker fixes the two worst things about power stations, Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu review – helmeted hero tangles with hateful Hutts in decent feature outing, and Understanding the modern cybercrime landscape.

mainItems.title: Anker fixes the two worst things about power stations
mainItems.summary: Anker's Solix S2000 solves two major pain points with power stations: idle power draw that can leave a battery unexpectedly dead, and the large footprint required to hold so much energy.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to state power, civil liberties, and anti-authoritarian concerns.

mainItems.title: Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu review – helmeted hero tangles with hateful Hutts in decent feature outing
mainItems.summary: The badass bounty hunter and his little green friend take on the Empire and Jabba the Hutt’s family in this solid enough addition to the ever-expanding universe Here is a non-canonical, or semi-canonical tale – maybe the distinction is…
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to state power, civil liberties, and anti-authoritarian concerns.

mainItems.title: Understanding the modern cybercrime landscape
mainItems.summary: Throughout 2025, HPE observed significant changes in how cybercriminals operate.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to systems, infrastructure, and technical risk.

mainItems.title: "We see this as the beginning of the end of the Apple Tax worldwide" - Fortnite returns to the App Store globally as Epic prepares for "the final battle"
mainItems.summary: Fortnite has returned to Apple's App Store around the world (*excluding Australia) as Epic Games proclaims "the beginning of the end of the Apple Tax worldwide". Read more
mainItems.whyItMatters: Worth your time if you care about design, criticism, and not just hype.

mainItems.title: Report: Nearly One-Third of Teachers Still Use ‘Discredited’ Reading Methods
mainItems.summary: While reform around reading instruction continues to gain momentum, about a third of teachers are using “discredited” methods to teach kids how to read and aren’t fully committed to the science of reading, a new report found.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Directly relevant to teaching, classroom practice, and educational judgment.

mainItems.title: Nintendo keeps finding new ways to reinvent platformers
mainItems.summary: In most platforming games, you're fighting against the world around you. You're trying to beat a level, nail a seemingly impossible series of jumps, or defeat a powerful boss.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to the shape of current AI systems and where the tooling is moving.

mainItems.title: The Download: Musk v. Altman, smart glasses for warfare, and Google I/O
mainItems.summary: This is today’s edition of The Download, our weekday newsletter that provides a daily dose of what’s going on in the world of technology.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Touches trust, surveillance, and the private use of AI systems.

mainItems.title: Steep drop in number of people with Affordable Care Act health coverage, analysis finds
mainItems.summary: Enrollment in Healthcare.gov and the other marketplaces is plunging by 5 million, the new paper from KFF finds. Last year, Congress failed to make a deal to keep the coverage more affordable.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to your current interest graph.

mainItems.title: Opinion: Students Are Digital Natives. Let Them Lead the AI Revolution in Education
mainItems.summary: For 30 years, American public schools have lived through the era of education reform: standards, accountability, assessments and increased public investment tied to measurable results.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Directly relevant to teaching, student agency, and school policy.

mainItems.title: Middle East crisis live: Iran warns it will ‘open new fronts’ against US if attacks resume after Trump suspends strikes
mainItems.summary: The US president said he called off a planned attack on Iran on Tuesday so that peace talks could continue Full report: Trump claims planned attack on Iran postponed after Tehran makes new proposal to end war Iran’s army has warned it…
mainItems.whyItMatters: Worth watching for authoritarian drift, selective enforcement, and democratic norms.

mainItems.title: Lawyer for Guy Who Sued Women Who Called Him ‘Psycho’ Caught Using AI
mainItems.summary: The attorney for Nikko D’Ambrosio, who tried and failed to sue women for posting about him in an “Are We Dating the Same Guy” Facebook group, has apparently been using AI to file non-existent citations, according to a judge.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to your current interest graph.

mainItems.title: What we know about how the U.S. government uses spyware (and what we don't)

new.title: Scavengers Reign
new.why: Animated speculative sci-fi with strong worldbuilding and a weirder imagination than most prestige SF.

new.title: Sugar
new.why: Noir detective structure with a genre wrinkle and a sensibility that feels adjacent to your crime/strangeness overlap.

new.title: The Lazarus Project
new.why: Time-loop espionage, moral pressure, and speculative plotting — very plausible fit.

new.title: Bodies
new.why: Time-spanning conspiracy/mystery structure that fits your taste for long-arc puzzle storytelling.

new.title: Constellation
new.why: A little more cerebral and mood-heavy, but it scratches the identity / reality-slippage itch.

new.title: The Devil’s Hour
new.why: Dark, twisty, and structurally ambitious without feeling like homework.

older.title: Counterpart
older.why: Probably the single cleanest “James show you may have missed”: espionage + parallel-world sci-fi + adult competence.

older.title: Patriot
older.why: Dry, strange, melancholy spy storytelling with a very distinct voice.

older.title: Utopia
older.why: Paranoid conspiracy energy, formal boldness, and a willingness to get strange and ugly.

older.title: Rubicon
older.why: A low-key conspiracy thriller that leans heavily on intelligence work, paranoia, and institutional atmosphere.

older.title: Continuum
older.why: A cleaner old-school sci-fi fit if you want procedural momentum wrapped around time-travel politics.

older.title: Person of Interest
older.why: Starts procedural and then quietly becomes one of the smartest AI / surveillance shows on television.

shared.title: Bad Sisters
shared.why: Darkly funny, well-observed, and ensemble-driven in a way that plausibly overlaps with Hacks / Derry Girls / Mare of Easttown energy.

shared.title: Detectorists
shared.why: Gentle, funny, humane, and specific — a softer shared-watch possibility.

shared.title: Astrid et Raphaëlle
shared.why: French procedural with a strong central duo and exactly the kind of foreign-crime appeal Maggie may share with you.

shared.title: Somebody Somewhere
shared.why: A warmer character-driven recommendation if the shared overlap leans more Bear / Good Place than pure detective work.
